citation: Rogge N, Rowley P. Is it all about the stars? Spending on star players and success in the National Football League. Omega. 2025. doi:10.1016/j.omega.2025.103433
abstract: This study examined whether spending on star players drives team success in the NFL from 2011 to 2020 using Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA). Four DEA models evaluated overall payroll efficiency, offensive star spending efficiency, defensive star spending efficiency, and quarterback spending efficiency across all 32 NFL teams. Star players were defined as those earning above the positional median salary. Efficiency scores were regressed against team success metrics including win-loss record, playoff appearances, and Super Bowl outcomes. The analysis found that payroll efficiency — not raw spending — was the primary driver of team success. Defensive star spending efficiency showed the strongest association with playoff and championship outcomes. Teams that efficiently allocated resources to defensive stars outperformed those that simply spent the most on offensive stars or quarterbacks.
tldr: Using Data Envelopment Analysis on 10 years of NFL salary and performance data, this study shows that it's not how much you spend on star players, but how efficiently you allocate that spending — particularly on the defensive side — that predicts playoff and Super Bowl success.
methods: Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) applied to salary cap and performance data for all 32 NFL teams from 2011 to 2020. Four DEA models constructed: overall payroll efficiency, offensive star spending, defensive star spending, and QB spending. Star player threshold defined as above positional median salary. Efficiency scores regressed against win-loss record, playoff appearances, and Super Bowl outcomes.
findings: Payroll efficiency was a stronger predictor of success than absolute spending levels. Defensive star spending efficiency had the strongest association with playoff appearances and Super Bowl wins. Teams with high overall DEA efficiency scores were more likely to reach the postseason. QB spending efficiency alone was not a significant predictor of team success.
limitations: Study relies on publicly available salary cap data which may not fully capture actual player compensation (signing bonuses, restructured deals). DEA models require assumptions about inputs/outputs. Causal inference is limited by observational design. Star player threshold based on median salary may not fully capture market value.
practicalImplications: NFL front offices and salary cap managers should prioritize efficiency in resource allocation over total spending. Investing heavily in a few marquee players at the expense of roster depth may be suboptimal. Defensive star efficiency appears to be a particularly high-leverage area for competitive success.
athleteDev: Highlights the organizational and roster-construction dimension of player development — identifying and retaining high-efficiency defensive contributors may yield greater team success than chasing the highest-priced offensive stars.

citation: Safar Cherati A, Khalifeh Soltani S, Moghadam N, et al. Is there a relationship between lower-extremity injuries and foot postures in professional football players? A prospective cohort study. Science and Medicine in Football. 2020. doi:10.1080/24733938.2020.1870711
abstract: This prospective cohort study investigated associations between foot posture and lower-extremity injuries in 420 Iranian professional football (soccer) players over the 2015–2016 season. Foot posture was assessed using four methods: the Foot Posture Index (FPI), optical foot scanning, static arch index, and navicular drop test. Players were followed throughout the season and 155 lower-extremity injuries were recorded. Pronated foot posture was associated with a relative risk of 1.8 to 2.3 for hamstring injuries. Abnormal foot posture was associated with medial collateral ligament (MCL) injuries. Flat foot was linked to time-loss injuries requiring absence from training or competition. The study supports routine foot posture assessment as part of pre-season injury screening in professional football.
tldr: In a season-long study of 420 professional football players, pronated foot posture increased hamstring injury risk nearly 2-fold and flat foot was linked to time-loss injuries — supporting pre-season foot screening as a standard injury risk assessment tool.
methods: Prospective cohort design. 420 Iranian professional football players assessed pre-season using four foot posture measurement tools: Foot Posture Index (FPI-6), optical foot scanning, static arch index, and navicular drop test. Players followed throughout the 2015–2016 competitive season. Lower-extremity injuries recorded by team medical staff. Relative risk (RR) calculated for injury associations by foot type.
findings: 155 lower-extremity injuries recorded over the season. Pronated foot posture associated with hamstring injuries (RR 1.8–2.3 depending on measurement tool). Abnormal foot posture (particularly pronation) associated with MCL injuries. Flat foot linked to time-loss injuries. No significant relationship between supinated foot type and specific injury categories. FPI and navicular drop test showed the strongest predictive associations.
limitations: Study conducted in Iranian professional football — generalizability to American football players is limited. Foot posture assessment methods vary in reliability. Study does not control for all confounding variables (e.g., training load, previous injury history). Injury definitions and recording methods may introduce ascertainment bias.
practicalImplications: Pre-season foot posture screening using FPI or navicular drop test can identify players at elevated risk for hamstring and MCL injuries. Athletes with pronated or flat foot morphology may benefit from orthotic interventions, targeted strength programming for intrinsic foot muscles, or modified loading progressions.
athleteDev: Foot posture screening should be integrated into annual physical evaluations for football athletes. Corrective interventions for pronated or flat foot types — including orthotic support and foot/ankle strengthening — may reduce lower-extremity injury burden across a season.
rtp: Abnormal foot posture (pronation, flat foot) is a risk factor for hamstring and MCL injuries. Post-injury evaluation should include foot posture reassessment, and RTP protocols may benefit from orthotic consultation to reduce reinjury risk.

citation: Luo EJ, Anastasio AT, Stauffer T, et al. Jones Fracture in the National Football League. Sports. 2024;12(1):7. doi:10.3390/sports12010007
abstract: This PRISMA-compliant systematic review examined outcomes of Jones fractures (proximal 5th metatarsal fractures) in NFL players. Six retrospective studies meeting inclusion criteria were analyzed, encompassing 285 Jones fractures. All identified players achieved return to play (RTP). Time to RTP ranged from 6 to 27 weeks depending on surgical technique and augmentation. Refracture rates ranged from 4% to 12%. The primary surgical treatment was intramedullary (IM) screw fixation, with some studies reporting use of bone marrow aspirate concentrate (BMAC) and demineralized bone matrix (DBM) as biological augmentation. The review found no statistically significant difference in RTP rates across surgical techniques, though augmentation may reduce refracture risk.
tldr: A systematic review of 285 Jones fractures in NFL players found a 100% return-to-play rate, with time to return ranging 6–27 weeks. Intramedullary screw fixation is the standard surgical approach, and biological augmentation (BMAC/DBM) may reduce the 4–12% refracture risk.
methods: PRISMA-compliant systematic review. Literature search of PubMed, MEDLINE, and Cochrane databases for studies reporting Jones fracture outcomes in NFL players. Six retrospective cohort studies met inclusion criteria. Data extracted on fracture classification, surgical technique, time to RTP, RTP rate, and refracture rate. Qualitative synthesis performed; meta-analysis not conducted due to heterogeneity.
findings: 285 Jones fractures identified across 6 studies. Return to play rate: 100%. Mean time to RTP: ranged 6–27 weeks across studies. Refracture rate: 4–12%. Primary surgical intervention: IM screw fixation. Biological augmentation (BMAC, DBM) used in some cases with potential reduction in refracture risk. No significant differences in RTP rates between surgical technique variations.
limitations: All included studies were retrospective in design, limiting causal inference. Heterogeneity in fracture classification, surgical technique, and outcome reporting precluded meta-analysis. Small sample sizes in individual studies. Selection bias possible as only published cases captured. Long-term outcomes (career longevity, chronic pain) not well reported.
practicalImplications: NFL medical staff can counsel players with Jones fractures that full return to play is expected with appropriate surgical management. Recovery timelines of 6–27 weeks should be communicated to coaches and front offices for roster planning. Biological augmentation should be considered to minimize refracture risk in high-demand positions.
athleteDev: Jones fractures are a known occupational risk in football given the cutting and lateral loading demands of the sport. Preventive strategies including footwear optimization, load monitoring, and bone health monitoring (Vitamin D, calcium) may reduce incidence in high-risk players.
rtp: 100% RTP expected following surgical IM screw fixation for Jones fractures in NFL players. RTP timeline: 6–27 weeks. Refracture risk 4–12% warrants post-RTP monitoring and consideration of biological augmentation at time of surgery. RTP criteria should include full weight-bearing, pain-free sport-specific movement, and imaging confirmation of healing.

citation: Johnson QR, Yang Y, Cabarkapa D, et al. Key Performance Indicators for College American Football Starters: An Exploratory Study. Journal of Functional Morphology and Kinesiology. 2025;10(1):19. doi:10.3390/jfmk10010019
abstract: This exploratory cross-sectional study profiled key performance indicators (KPIs) in 16 NCAA Division-II college American football starters during pre-season 2022. Measures included body composition (InBody 270 BIA), joint kinematics during bilateral squat using the DARI markerless motion capture system, isometric mid-thigh pull (IMTP) on force plates, countermovement jump (CMJ), and multi-rebound jump (MRJ). Significant position-group differences were found in body composition (p=0.004), muscular strength (p=0.01), and muscular power (p=0.03). No significant differences were found in joint kinematics between position groups, suggesting movement quality may be a shared KPI across all starters regardless of position. The study provides a reference dataset for position-specific performance profiling in college football.
tldr: In 16 NCAA Division-II football starters, body composition, muscular strength (IMTP), and power (CMJ/MRJ) differed significantly by position group, but joint kinematics during bilateral squat did not — suggesting movement quality is a universal performance standard while strength and power benchmarks should be position-specific.
methods: Cross-sectional observational design. 16 NCAA Division-II American football starters assessed during 2022 pre-season. Body composition: InBody 270 bioelectrical impedance analysis. Joint kinematics: DARI markerless motion capture system during bilateral squat. Muscular strength: isometric mid-thigh pull (IMTP) on dual force plates. Muscular power: countermovement jump (CMJ) and multi-rebound jump (MRJ) on force plates. One-way ANOVA with post-hoc testing used to assess position group differences.
findings: Significant position-group differences in body composition (p=0.004), muscular strength via IMTP (p=0.01), and muscular power via CMJ/MRJ (p=0.03). No significant differences in joint kinematics between position groups (p>0.05). Linemen showed greatest body mass and IMTP peak force; skill positions showed greatest relative power output. Movement quality (bilateral squat kinematics) was comparable across all starter position groups.
limitations: Small sample (n=16) limits statistical power and generalizability. Single institution NCAA Division-II cohort — findings may not apply to Power Five or NFL-level athletes. DARI system reliability in field settings not fully established. Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference or longitudinal tracking.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning staff should use position-specific benchmarks for body composition, IMTP, and CMJ/MRJ when evaluating college football athletes. Joint kinematics quality targets (bilateral squat) can be applied universally across positions. The DARI markerless system provides a practical, rapid field-based movement screen for team settings.
athleteDev: This study supports a position-specific approach to strength and power development benchmarks while using universal movement quality standards. Pre-season profiling using IMTP, CMJ, MRJ, and markerless motion capture can identify developmental gaps in college football starters and guide individualized training prescriptions.
rtp: IMTP, CMJ, and MRJ force-plate assessments provide objective strength and power benchmarks that can anchor RTP criteria for injured college football players. Joint kinematics screening via markerless motion capture offers a practical, no-marker-required movement quality assessment for post-injury clearance.
